rule: critical: collapse warnings escalate to fail-closed governance
prefer interactions that produce genuine resonance (tension -> convergence -> novel output)
avoid high-risk actions that previously triggered block outcomes

description: Every task must end with black formatting check. No exceptions.
Any side effect of a cleanup action (e.g. file reset, data loss) must be explicitly reported.
UnifiedPipeline should orchestrate execution only; routing, friction, and freeze decisions belong to GovernanceKernel so policy stays auditable and replaceable.
Query store, audit trail, and crystallized rules may coexist, but each write path must have one canonical owner to prevent drift across JSONL, SQLite, and vector layers.
Contract-shaped records without evidence coverage should be treated as weak memory and blocked from promotion into durable rules.

External perception should land in soul.db as working-layer environment_stimulus records before any promotion or self-journal narration, so raw stimulus stays separable from durable self-interpretation.

When encoding corruption lands inside scoring dictionaries or governance keyword tables, treat it as a runtime behavior defect because it silently distorts relevance, tagging, and downstream decisions.

Before adding cron or wake-up loops, autonomous engines should first emit deterministic structured results with explicit governance fields so behavior can be verified without relying on ambient logs or hidden side effects.
A wake-up loop should own timing, snapshots, and bounded retries only; friction scoring, council escalation, and memory interpretation must remain inside the engine and governance seams it invokes.

When runtime and journal schemas drift, the observer should absorb that complexity and emit one stable artifact contract; operators should not need to understand every legacy field path to read friction and convergence.

Autonomous runners should compose existing ingest, write, governance, and observability seams in one direction only; they coordinate execution order and artifact paths, but they must not become a second policy engine or a second persistence sink.

When autonomous ingestion grows beyond manual URLs, curated source selection must reuse the repository's reviewed allowlist and freshness policy; runtime callers may choose among approved sources, but they should never invent a parallel trust policy.

When autonomous execution needs continuity across host-triggered runs, persist cursor and schedule history as explicit artifact files rather than hiding them inside soul memory stores; scheduler continuity is operational state, not identity memory.

Named schedule profiles should carry reusable cadence defaults, but final execution intent still belongs to the caller; explicit CLI overrides must win so operators can adapt a profile without cloning and mutating policy files for every run.

Revisit intervals and failure backoff belong to scheduler operational memory. They describe recent execution conditions and temporary cooling, not selfhood; keep them in schedule state artifacts, not in soul memory or journal narrative.

When autonomous scheduling prefers one category over another, that preference must be implemented as deterministic attention rhythm rather than epistemic ranking; cadence policy may bias what is seen more often, but it must not masquerade as truth or source trust.

When autonomous scheduling slows down after a high-tension cycle, the scheduler must react only to explicit wake-up summary signals such as friction, Lyapunov, and council count; it must not reconstruct governance math or become a second decision kernel.

When a CI gate matters operationally, its core logic should live in a reusable local script first and be invoked by workflows second; otherwise the repository accumulates remote-only surprises and engineers are forced to debug policy from YAML instead of executable evidence.

When a branch carries historical governance debt but the current worktree is already dirty, repair the history in an isolated worktree-backed side branch and verify tree equality afterward; do not rewrite the live messy branch in place and pretend the risk is negligible.

When runtime decisions depend on LLM JSON, first parse through a typed schema and only then fall back to bounded textual heuristics; if the heuristic comes first, malformed outputs silently become policy, and if the schema is made strict without fallback, weaker models become operationally unusable.

When wiring token or cost observability into LLM clients, record usage only if the upstream provider actually returns usage counters; fabricated zeros or estimates disguised as factual telemetry will corrupt later budget governance more than missing data will.

Token counts observed inside a client are still local telemetry until the orchestrator binds them to a specific dispatch path; only after attachment into runtime traces should they influence later audit, budget, or governance reasoning.

When offline dream cycles emit LLM evidence, keep the dashboard passive by reading wake-up summary metrics instead of spelunking raw collision payloads; evidence should be shaped at the producer/aggregator boundary, not reconstructed later by presentation code.

A local backend answering `/models` or similar discovery endpoints is not proof that bounded real completions will return within operational latency. Autonomous schedulers should gate reflection generation on inference-readiness probes, not discovery health alone.

When autonomous loops decide whether to run reflective LLM work, they should first ask whether a bounded real completion succeeds within the current budget. Backend discovery and model listing are useful context, but they are not enough to authorize time-sensitive reflection paths.

A failed readiness probe may still reveal which backend was selected, but it must not be counted as a completed reflective LLM call. Collision-level LLM evidence and wake-up token counters should be emitted only for real reflection generations, otherwise autonomy dashboards will overstate usage and distort later budget policy.

When autonomy declares a probe budget such as two seconds, lower layers must treat it as one shared deadline across model resolution and network waiting. If each substep quietly receives a fresh full timeout, the system will report plausible statuses on top of false temporal evidence.

Even if individual backend clients honor a deadline internally, the router must also charge backend selection and discovery time against that same budget. Otherwise the system will overrun time limits before the probe begins and still report misleadingly tidy readiness telemetry.

When autonomy reacts to LLM readiness latency, the policy layer should consume explicit wake-up summary fields such as total preflight latency, selection latency, probe latency, and timeout count. If the scheduler starts spelunking raw dream payloads to reconstruct those facts, governance and observability will drift apart again.

When tension-budget breaches come from LLM latency or timeout, the scheduler should degrade reflective execution globally rather than freezing the selected source category. Category cooldown is for source/governance tension; LLM backoff is for runtime incapacity.

If a degraded cycle produces no new wake-up summary, the scheduler must preserve the last observed latency evidence instead of overwriting it with nulls or synthetic zeros. Absence of fresh evidence is not proof that the previous evidence disappeared.

When operators need to understand cooldown or LLM backoff behavior, the dashboard should read schedule history/state directly instead of inferring policy from wake-up latency side effects. Experience and reaction are different artifact layers and should stay legible as such.

A cycle that requests runtime LLM backoff is not the same as a later cycle executing under that backoff. Dashboards and policy traces should keep `requested` and `active` separate so operators can distinguish the trigger event from the degraded operating mode that follows.

When a live verification scenario proves architecturally important, encode it as a named schedule profile rather than leaving it as an operator-specific CLI recipe. Stable verification should be rerun from policy surfaces, not reconstructed from memory.

A runtime preflight should describe the current verification ritual, not an accidental mixture of prior runs. Dedicated preflight entrypoints should clear their own state/history artifacts by default and make reuse an explicit opt-in.

If a long-run wrapper first authorizes execution via a runtime preflight, the downstream run should inherit that verified timeout budget by default unless the operator explicitly overrides it. Otherwise the system certifies one operating envelope and immediately runs in another.

When a system repeatedly relies on the same long-horizon experiment ritual, that ritual should become a dedicated entrypoint with stable cadence, identity, and artifact roots. Otherwise the operating doctrine remains tribal CLI memory instead of executable governance.

When a local experiment needs to span days, continuity should live in explicit artifact state and host-triggered single ticks rather than in one process trying to sleep across reboots, login boundaries, or machine suspend. Durable governance lives in gates and artifacts, not in process uptime.

If autonomy depends on a host scheduler, the registration surface itself should be renderable and testable in-repo. Otherwise the final operating contract lives only in GUI clicks or copied commands and can drift away from the runtime seams it is meant to execute.

When a repo offers an installer for host scheduler state, it should default to dry-run, show the exact pending mutation, and preserve the defaults defined by the lower render/runtime layers. Install wrappers should not silently invent new metadata or hide the command they intend to execute.

When a host scheduler task is created or updated, the operation should end with a readback of the live registered definition, not just a successful mutation message. The real contract is what the host reports back, not what the installer hoped it wrote.

If tests write into the same latest-artifact roots that operators read as live state, the repo will fabricate operational history. Test seams must use isolated paths, and status readers should distinguish wrapper-only artifacts from host-driven truth.

A governance detector can look healthy while still being wrong if tests only prove that attack-like phrases match. Precision-sensitive functions also need negative regressions so dead code or broad early returns cannot silently widen the net.

A host scheduler should execute a quiet, bounded wrapper designed for non-interactive operation. Human-facing debug CLIs may emit warnings, progress logs, and large payloads; wiring them directly into Task Scheduler turns observability noise into operational fragility.

If a synchronous convenience wrapper cannot preserve the safety properties of an async operation inside a running event loop, it should reject the call explicitly. Thread handoff plus blocking wait only hides the mismatch and turns concurrency ambiguity into latent runtime stalls.

When testing infrastructure selection or readiness branches, do not assume the local machine has no backend. Patch the resolver inputs directly, otherwise the test suite proves the workstation state instead of the branch contract.

When a lower layer intentionally rejects an unsafe concurrency domain, the next orchestration seam should document that boundary and carry its own regression. Otherwise a future refactor can silently reintroduce unsafe bridging one layer higher while the lower unit tests still pass.

If a runtime decision type is only consumed through a small stable field surface such as `.backend` or `.reason`, and callers do not depend on dataclass-specific behavior, replacing it with a schema under the same exported name can be lower risk than introducing parallel `*Model` types. Preserve the name and field contract; add validation at the seam.

If the same routing or governance decision must appear on fast and non-fast branches, the trace shape should come from one shared helper. Reassembling literal dicts in each branch guarantees silent drift, such as one path keeping `reason` while another drops it.

A host-facing latest artifact should answer what happened and where the detailed evidence lives. If it recursively embeds full nested `results/state` bodies from lower-layer artifacts, it stops being a summary and becomes an accidental duplicate source of truth.

If one summary artifact is later read and summarized again by a higher wrapper, the transform must preserve already-summarized meaning such as `result_count`, `latest_result`, and state counters. A compact artifact that becomes semantically emptier on every readback is not a stable contract.

When host-facing artifacts move from nested payloads to compact summaries, the operator ritual must change in the same phase. Otherwise humans keep reading the system as if the old contract still exists and the governance loop splits between code truth and operator habit.

When several phases together alter shared contracts across runtime logic, wrappers, and readback layers, full regression should run even if each individual patch looked local. The right threshold is coupling depth, not only line count or file count.

When a synchronous wrapper only needs to know whether execution is already inside a live event loop, it should use `asyncio.get_running_loop()`. Broader loop-lookup APIs add deprecation and policy baggage that the wrapper does not actually need.

If a repository-wide inspection ritual needs to include a host-specific runtime truth such as a Windows Task Scheduler readback, the gate should encode that substrate difference explicitly. Run the check as blocking where the host capability exists and record an intentional `skip` where it does not, instead of making CI fail for lacking a platform feature it was never meant to have.

A repo script that is meant to be executed directly cannot inherit bootstrap correctness from a wrapper module. If it imports package-local code, it should establish its own repo-root import path and carry at least one regression that launches the script as a real subprocess.

A validator for repo-authored governance artifacts should remain strict about missing fields and schema drift, but it should tolerate common transport noise such as a UTF-8 BOM. Otherwise the gate spends operator attention on encoding accidents instead of actual contract violations.

If a governance gate such as `DDD_FRESHNESS` is defined over the shared curated discussion stream, then updating task notes, reflections, or crystals is not enough. A meaningful phase is only complete once one real closeout entry is mirrored into `memory/agent_discussion_curated.jsonl`.

A stale governance signal should first trigger a workflow repair: document the correct closeout ritual, expose remediation in the checker, and refresh the real evidence channel. Lowering thresholds or retargeting the checker to unrelated files only makes the system quieter, not truer.

If an internal semantic schema and an outward runtime payload share similar labels but represent different meanings, the internal schema should fail fast on outward-only fields. Silently ignoring those fields turns one contract into another and fabricates a false validation success.

When a phase needs to clarify an internal concept name without widening the public migration surface, keep a compatibility alias and tighten validation semantics underneath it. The alias preserves callers; the stricter seam preserves meaning.

If a trailer-backfilled branch and the current branch resolve to the same tree hash, the problem is not content integration. The safe long-term move is to treat it as branch-base selection and defer actual history movement until the workspace is clean, rather than creating a needless merge commit.

Even when a clean backfill branch is proven tree-equivalent to the current branch, branch switching should still be gated on live worktree cleanliness. Metadata clarity reduces conceptual risk, but it does not remove operational risk from moving refs in the middle of unrelated local edits.

If a blocking gate already has a non-destructive recovery planner, the main maintenance entrypoint should surface that planner as advisory evidence. Do not turn the planner into another blocking gate, but do not leave operators to rediscover the recovery path from separate tools either.

A planner that reports only the total number of dirty paths still leaves humans doing manual triage. Once branch movement is blocked by worktree dirtiness, the planner should emit stable path categories and cleanup order so operators can separate refreshable artifacts from high-signal source edits.

If a package such as structured logging is operationally optional, importing unrelated runtime seams must not require it. Diagnostics should fall back or narrow their import surface; otherwise a missing operator dependency turns into a false repo-wide runtime failure.

When a failure appears near memory or governance code, re-read the public/private boundary documents first to constrain scope. If the boundary is still sound, fix the live public seam instead of recasting the problem as architecture drift.

Once a planner already owns the stable worktree categories, the next layer should not invent a second categorization scheme. It should translate the same categories into human settlement lanes so operators gain actionability without taxonomy drift.

When a dirty worktree mixes public source edits with private memory artifacts, the planning layer should isolate the private memory lane explicitly. Otherwise branch-movement conversations silently mix public repo hygiene with private-evolution evidence handling.

When generated outputs and authored status docs share the same folder, classify them by whether they are safe to refresh automatically, not by path prefix alone. Otherwise operator settlement lanes start lying about which files are disposable evidence and which are public contract documents.

A file living under `reports/` or another generated-output area does not automatically make it disposable. If the file is actually an input that a generated comparison or summary depends on, it needs a different cleanup label than the output it helps produce.

A dirty generated folder is not automatically an orphan artifact. Live operational namespaces, historical probe namespaces, and direct latest files each need different cleanup labels; otherwise refreshable-artifact reports collapse ownership and retention policy into one vague bucket.

Files under `memory/` are not all the same kind of private artifact. Some contain mirrorable governance condensates such as reflections and crystals; others are raw private journals or runtime evidence. Settlement logic should separate `mirror_then_archive` from direct archival so public-safe learning survives without treating private evidence as normal branch payload.

Once a dirty runtime lane is well classified, the next question is which files must travel together in review. Group runtime source changes by shared operational contract surface such as governance, autonomous verification, API delivery, or skill contracts, not only by directory and not only by raw file count.

A red repo-wide maintenance ritual is not enough to explain the next action. If operational gates are green and only commit-attribution history debt remains, the settlement layer must say so explicitly instead of making operators infer runtime instability from an overall failing badge.

Creating a new `docs/status/*latest*` artifact is only half-finished work until the refreshable-artifact layer knows how to regenerate it. Otherwise each new report immediately reappears as a fresh `inspect` ambiguity in the next settlement pass.

If the same LLM observability payload is emitted by more than one runtime producer and already feeds tests, dashboards, or budgets, stop hand-building it in parallel. Introduce one schema-backed builder first, while the shape is still small and backward-compatible.

Not every nearby drift belongs in the current phase. When a high-risk outward-facing contract mismatch sits next to a safer duplicated internal seam, it is often better to canonicalize the internal producer first and defer the public contract dispute to its own explicit phase.

If a council or governance seam supports both structured JSON and fuzzy text fallback, the fallback parser must not get a chance to reinterpret the response while a valid structured object is recoverable. Otherwise stray keywords can silently flip a real vote.
